content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Martin Huff
Executive Director
Franchise Tax Board

Date : June 15, 1979

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Barbara Campbell [illegible]

Subject: Government Code Section 84210(h) and
April 2, 1979, advice letter

At its June 6, 1979, meeting, the Fair Political Practices
Commission considered your June 4, 1979, memorandum expressing
your disagreement with the interpretation given by the staff
of the Fair Political Practices Commission in its April 2,
1979, advice letter to Robert Summers of the Franchise Tax
Board's Political Reform Audit Division.

The Commission agreed with the advice outlined in the
April 2, 1979, advice letter. As noted in that letter, the
Commission has proposed a legislative change for that section.

State of California                                                  Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To    :  Thomas Houston                          Date  :  June 4, 1979
        Chairman, Fair Political Practices Commission
        1100 K Street Building                    File No4 /3
        Sacramento, CA  95814
                                                  Telephone: ATSS (
                                                             ( 916 )355-0413

From  :  Martin Huff

Subject:  Government Code Section 84210(h)


        Government Code Section 84210(h) provides that the amount of each separate
expenditure to each person be reported on campaign statements. In conflict
with this section, however, is the advice presently being disseminated by
the staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission. That is, it is accept-
able for a filer to report only the total or lump sum amount of expenditures
made to each payee.

        It has been and is a long standing rule of statutory construction that when
a statute is unambiguous, it requires no interpretation. As the extent
Section 84210(h) is specific and uncontestable, the authority of the Commission
or its employees to promulgate an interpretation that deviates from the
clear meaning of the section under consideration is completely questionable
and without foundation.

        Although the foregoing staff interpretation of Section 84210(h) may be in-
cluded in pending legislation, the present Commission position can only be
categorized as amending the law without the benefit of the legislative process.
Such a situation is unacceptable and one that warrants and has this depart-
ment's total opposition.

        In the course of continued implementation of the Political Reform Act, it re-
mains our opinion that the entire initiative, including Section 84210(h),
should be applied as written. To acquiesce in a contrary administrative
directive not only forces the Franchise Tax Board to ignore present law but
also constitutes an unacceptable acknowledgment of the supersedence by the
Fair Political Practices Commission of legislative prerogative and authority.


[signature]

Executive Officer

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance                Administration • • •       Executive/Legal • • •        Enforcement • • •    Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662                      322-5660                      322-5901                      322-6441                322-6444

April 2, 1979


Robert C. Summers
Political Reform Audit
Division
Franchise Tax Board
1001 G Street, Suite 200
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Bob:

     The staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission
has considered the question posed by Vigo C. Nielsen, Jr.,
in his March 15, 1979, letter to David Hudson of your office.
Mr. Nielsen asked whether there had to be a separate listing
of each payment made to a payee on a committee's campaign
statement.

     The staff's interpretation of Government Code Section
84210(h) has been that it is the total amount of expenditures
for each payee that must be reported on the campaign state-
ment. We recognize that this interpretation can be read as
conflicting with the express language of Government Code
Section 84210(h) which requires disclosure of "the amount
of each separate expenditure to each person." However, such
disclosure does not further the purposes of the Act. Further-
more this interpretation has been embodied in each of our */
campaign disclosure manuals and examples contained therein.
Therefore should any of your auditors be asked whether a
committee is required to report each separate expenditure
to a payee, the answer should be no.

     This interpretation will be clarified in the statute
in our legislative proposal revising the contents of campaign
statement section.


_____________________________

*/ For 1975, see pp. 9-11; for 1976, see pp. 14-15;
for 1977, see pp. 16-17; for 1978, see pp. 25-26; and for
1979, see pp. 25-26 of the respective campaign disclosure
manuals.

Robert C. Summers
April 2, 1979
Page Two


I hope this will not inconvenience you or your office.
If you have any other questions, please feel free to call.

Sincerely,

Barbara
Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

BC:plh

cc: Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

DOBES & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law
SUITE 2500, ONE BULL BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111
TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

March 15, 1979

Mr. David Hudson
California Franchise Tax Board
Political Reform Audit Division
1001 G Street, Suite 200
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Disclosure Required for
Expenditures to Same Payee

Dear Dave:

During recent post-audit conferences, you
have informed attorneys in this office that the Franchise
Tax Board interprets Government Code Section 84210(h)
to require separate listings of each payment to the
same payee made during a single reporting period. You
explained that this is based on the statute's requirement to report the payee "together with the amount of
each separate expenditure to each person during the
period covered by the campaign statement." You explained
that if a payee made 50 payments to PSA of $100 each,
the statute requires 50 separate listings. We have
been advising our clients to disclose these 50 payments
as a single entry for $5,000.

We respectfully disagree with the FTB interpretation. Unlike laws prior to Waxman-Dymally that
only identified the payees by name but not amount, we
interpret 84210(h) to require disclosure of each payee
with the total amount to that payee during the period.
The Fair Political Practices Commission legal staff
agrees with our interpretation, as described on pages 25
and 26 of their 1978 Information Manual, which I think
was approved by the Franchise Tax Board. The Manual
explains that the payee must be identified "together
with the amount of the expenditures" (emphasis added).
Barbara Campbell of the FPPC legal staff told me on
March 6, 1978, that separate listings of each expenditure to the same payee was not required.

You have explained that your working papers
for these audits will show these aggregations to the
same payee as technical errors, but because FTB considers
them as "insubstantial exceptions," they will not

SACRAMENTO OFFICE: SUITE 550, 1228 EIGHTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • (916) 446-6792

Mr. David Hudson
March 15, 1973
Page 2


appear on the public audit. Since we believe our
clients' reports are correct in this respect and your
working papers should reflect this, we request that the
FTB first discuss your interpretation with the FPPC.

Thank you for your consideration in this
matter; we would appreciate knowing the outcome of this
inquiry.

Very truly yours,

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:pmr
cc: John H. Hodgson, Esq.

sections.question_synthetic: Does Government Code Section 84210(h) require reporting each separate expenditure individually, or does FPPC's staff guidance allowing lump sum reporting of expenditures to each payee comply with the statute?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence/complaint letter, not an FPPC response. No formal question/conclusion sections present. Document is dated June 4, 1979 and addressed to the FPPC Chairman. Significant OCR artifacts and page numbering suggest partial scan of document. Writer criticizes FPPC interpretation of campaign reporting requirements as exceeding agency authority.

embedding.summary: Martin Huff from the Franchise Tax Board writes to challenge FPPC staff guidance that permits filers to report lump sum expenditures by payee, arguing this conflicts with the unambiguous language of Government Code Section 84210(h) which requires each separate expenditure to be reported.
